subject: Physics
q: A thin cylindrical rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed horizontally on the principle axis of a concave mirror of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The rod is placed in a such a way that mid point of the rod is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole of mirror. The length of the image formed by the mirror will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: A thin cylindrical rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed horizontally on the principle axis of a concave mirror of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The rod is placed in a such a way that mid point of the rod is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole of mirror. The length of the image formed by the mirror will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
As per sign conventions, object distance uA=-45 cm, focal length f=-20 cm.

From the mirror formula

1vA+1uA=1f⇒1vA+1-45=1-20⇒vA=-45×(-20)-45-(-20)=-90025⇒vA=-36 cm

And uB=-35 cm

1vB+1uB=1f⇒1vB+1-35=1-20⇒vB=-35×(-20)-35-(-20)=700-15⇒vB=-1403 cm

Now, length of image=vA-vB

=-36+1403 cm

=-108+1403 cm

=323 cm

∴ x=32

subject: Physics
q:
In finding out refractive index of glass slab the following observations were made through travelling microscope 50 vernier scale division $=49 \mathrm{MSD} ; 20$ divisions on main scale in each $\mathrm{cm}$
For mark on paper
$\mathrm{MSR}=8.45 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{VC}=26$
For mark on paper seen through slab
MSR $=7.12 \mathrm{~cm}, V C=41$
For powder particle on the top surface of the glass slab
$\mathrm{MSR}=4.05 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{VC}=1$
(MSR $=$ Main Scale Reading, VC $=$ Vernier Coincidence)
Refractive index of the glass slab is :
question:
In finding out refractive index of glass slab the following observations were made through travelling microscope 50 vernier scale division $=49 \mathrm{MSD} ; 20$ divisions on main scale in each $\mathrm{cm}$
For mark on paper
$\mathrm{MSR}=8.45 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{VC}=26$
For mark on paper seen through slab
MSR $=7.12 \mathrm{~cm}, V C=41$
For powder particle on the top surface of the glass slab
$\mathrm{MSR}=4.05 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{VC}=1$
(MSR $=$ Main Scale Reading, VC $=$ Vernier Coincidence)
Refractive index of the glass slab is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 1 \mathrm{MSD}=\frac{1 \mathrm{~cm}}{20}=0.05 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & 1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{49}{50} \mathrm{MSD}=\frac{49}{50} \times 0.05 \mathrm{~cm}=0.049 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD}=0.001 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$
For mark on paper, $\mathrm{L}_1=8.45 \mathrm{~cm}+26 \times 0.001 \mathrm{~cm}$ $=84.76 \mathrm{~mm}$ For mark on paper through slab, $\mathrm{L} 2=7.12 \mathrm{~cm}+$ $41 \times 0.001 \mathrm{~cm}=71.61 \mathrm{~mm}$ For powder particle on top surface, $\mathrm{ZE}=4.05 \mathrm{~cm}$ $+1 \times 0.001 \mathrm{~cm}=40.51 \mathrm{~mm}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \text { actual } \mathrm{L}_1=84.76-40.51=44.25 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \text { actual } \mathrm{L}_2=71.61-40.51=31.10 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \mathrm{~L}_2=\frac{\mathrm{L}_1}{\mu} \\ \Rightarrow & \mu=\frac{\mathrm{L}_1}{\mathrm{~L}_2}=\frac{44.25}{31.10}=1.42\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A light ray falls on a square glass slab as shown in the diagram. The index of refraction of the glass, if total internal reflection is to occur at the vertical face, is equal to :
question: A light ray falls on a square glass slab as shown in the diagram. The index of refraction of the glass, if total internal reflection is to occur at the vertical face, is equal to :
solution:
At point A by Snell's law $$ \mu=\frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin r} \Rightarrow \sin r=\frac{1}{\mu \sqrt{2}} $$ At point $\mathrm{B}$, for total internal reflection, $$ \sin i_1=\frac{1}{\mu} $$
From figure, $i_1=90^{\circ}-r$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore\left(\sin 90^{\circ}-r\right)=\frac{1}{\mu} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos r=\frac{1}{\mu} \end{aligned} $$ Now $\cos r=\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 r}=\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{2 \mu^2}}$ $$ =\sqrt{\frac{2 \mu^2-1}{2 \mu^2}} $$ From eqs (ii) and (iii) $$ \frac{1}{\mu}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mu^2-1}{2 \mu^2}} $$ Squaring both sides and then solving, we get $$ \mu=\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} $$

subject: Physics
q: An object is located in a fixed position in front of a screen. Sharp image is obtained on the screen for two positions of a thin lens separated by 10 $\mathrm{cm}$. The size of the images in two situations are in the ratio $3: 3$. What is the distance between the screen and the object?
question: An object is located in a fixed position in front of a screen. Sharp image is obtained on the screen for two positions of a thin lens separated by 10 $\mathrm{cm}$. The size of the images in two situations are in the ratio $3: 3$. What is the distance between the screen and the object?
solution:
Given: Separation of lens for two of its position, $d=10 \mathrm{~cm}$ Ratio of size of the images in two positions
$$
\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{3}{2}
$$
Distance of object from the screen, $D=$ ? Applying formula,
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{(D+d)^2}{(D-d)^2} \\
&\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3}{2}=\frac{(D+10)^2}{(D-10)^2} \\
&\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3}{2}=\frac{D^2+100+20 D}{D^2+100-20 D} \\
&\Rightarrow 3 D^2+300-60 D=2 D^2+200+40 D \\
&\Rightarrow D^2-100 D+100=0
\end{aligned}
$$
On solving, we get $D=99 \mathrm{~cm}$
Hence the distance between the screen and the object is $99 \mathrm{~cm}$.

question: To determine refractive index of glass slab using a travelling microscope, minimum number of readings required are :

Reading two ⇒ with slab

Reading three ⇒ with Lycopodium powder

subject: Physics
q: If the refractive index of the material of a prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cot</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is the angle of prism then the angle of minimum deviation will be
question: If the refractive index of the material of a prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cot</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is the angle of prism then the angle of minimum deviation will be
solution:
The formula to calculate the refractive index μ of the material of the prism is given by

μ=sinA+δm2sinA2   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

cotA2=sinA+δm2sinA2

⇒cosA2=sinA+δm2⇒sinπ2-A2=sinA+δm2

⇒A+δm2=π2-A2

⇒δm=π-2 A

subject: Physics
q: A plano-convex lens having radius of curvature of first surface 2 cm exhibits focal length of $f_1$ in air. Another plano-convex lens with first surface radius of curvature 3 cm has focal length of $\mathrm{f}_2$ when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.2 . If both the lenses are made of same glass of refractive index 1.5 , the ratio of $f_1$ and $f_2$ will be
question: A plano-convex lens having radius of curvature of first surface 2 cm exhibits focal length of $f_1$ in air. Another plano-convex lens with first surface radius of curvature 3 cm has focal length of $\mathrm{f}_2$ when it is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.2 . If both the lenses are made of same glass of refractive index 1.5 , the ratio of $f_1$ and $f_2$ will be
solution:
For case (1)
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1.5}{v_1}-\frac{1}{\propto}=\frac{(1.5-1)}{R_1} \\ & \frac{1}{f_1}-\frac{1.5}{v_1}=\frac{1-1.5}{\propto}\end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{(1.5-1)}{R_1}$ ...(1) $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{1}{2 R_1}$ For case (2) $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1.5}{v_1}-\frac{1.2}{\infty}=\frac{(1.5-1.2)}{R_2} \\ & \frac{1.2}{f_2}-\frac{1.5}{v_1}=\frac{(1.2-1.5)}{\propto} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1.2}{f_2}=\frac{0.3}{R_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{4 R_2} \end{aligned}$ So clearly, $\frac{f_1}{f_2}=\frac{2 \times 2}{4 \times 3}=\frac{1}{3}$

subject: Physics
q:
Distance between object and its image $\left( \text{magnified by } -\frac{1}{3}\right)$ is 30 cm. The focal length of the mirror used is $\left(\frac{x}{4}\right) \mathrm{cm}$,
where magnitude of value of $x$ is _________.
question:
Distance between object and its image $\left( \text{magnified by } -\frac{1}{3}\right)$ is 30 cm. The focal length of the mirror used is $\left(\frac{x}{4}\right) \mathrm{cm}$,
where magnitude of value of $x$ is _________.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & M=-\frac{1}{3} \\ & -\frac{-V}{-U}=\frac{-1}{3} \Rightarrow V=\frac{U}{3}\end{aligned}$
Distance $b / w$ object and image :

$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}-\mathrm{V}=30 \\ & \mathrm{U}-\frac{\mu}{3}=30 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{U}=45 \quad \mathrm{~V}=15 \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~V}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{U}}=-\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{45} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{~F}=\frac{45}{4} \\ & \mathrm{x}=45\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The image of an illuminated square is obtained on a screen with the help of a converging lens. The distance of the square from the lens is 40 $\mathrm{cm}$. The area of the image is 9 times that of the square. The focal length of the lens is :
question: The image of an illuminated square is obtained on a screen with the help of a converging lens. The distance of the square from the lens is 40 $\mathrm{cm}$. The area of the image is 9 times that of the square. The focal length of the lens is :
solution:
If side of object square $=\ell$ and side of image square $=\ell^{\prime}$
From question, $\frac{\ell^{\prime 2}}{\ell}=9$
or $\frac{\ell^{\prime}}{\ell}=3$
i.e., magnification $\mathrm{m}=3$
$\mathrm{u}=-40 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{v}=3 \times 40=120 \mathrm{~cm}$
$$
\mathrm{f}=?
$$
From formula, $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{120}-\frac{1}{-40}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \\
& \text { or, } \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{120}+\frac{1}{40}=\frac{1+3}{120} \therefore \mathrm{f}=30 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The value of numerical aperture of the objective lens of a microscope is <math><mn>1.25</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If light of wavelength <math><mn>5000</mn><mi> </mi><mi>Å</mi></math> is used, the minimum separation between two points, to be seen as distinct, will be:
question: The value of numerical aperture of the objective lens of a microscope is <math><mn>1.25</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If light of wavelength <math><mn>5000</mn><mi> </mi><mi>Å</mi></math> is used, the minimum separation between two points, to be seen as distinct, will be:
solution: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>0.61</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>0.61</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>5000</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mn>1.25</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>0.24</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> forms an image of an extended source of light on a photoelectric cell. A current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is produced. The lens is replaced by another convex lens having the same diameter but focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The photoelectric current now is
question: A convex lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> forms an image of an extended source of light on a photoelectric cell. A current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is produced. The lens is replaced by another convex lens having the same diameter but focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The photoelectric current now is
solution:
For a light of any given frequency, the photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of light.

As the diameter of the lens remains the same, both lenses will collect the same amount of light. Therefore, the intensity of the light will remain the same.

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Physics
q: A beam of light consisting of red, green and blue colours is incident on a right-angled prism on face $A B$. The refractive indices of the material for the above red, green and blue colours are $1.39,1.44$ and $1.47$ respectively. A person looking on surface $A C$ of the prism will see
question: A beam of light consisting of red, green and blue colours is incident on a right-angled prism on face $A B$. The refractive indices of the material for the above red, green and blue colours are $1.39,1.44$ and $1.47$ respectively. A person looking on surface $A C$ of the prism will see
solution:
For light to come out through face 'AC', total internal reflection must not take place.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { i.e., } \theta < c \Rightarrow \sin \theta < \sin c \\
& \Rightarrow \sin \theta < \frac{1}{\mu}
\end{aligned}
$$
or $\quad \mu < \frac{1}{\sin \theta} \Rightarrow \mu < \frac{1}{\sin 45^{\circ}}$
$$
\Rightarrow \mu < \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow \mu < 1.414
$$

subject: Physics
q: In a microscope the objective is having focal length $f_{o}=2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and eye-piece is having focal length $f_{e}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The tube length is 32 cm. The magnification produced by this microscope for normal adjustment is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: In a microscope the objective is having focal length $f_{o}=2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and eye-piece is having focal length $f_{e}=4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The tube length is 32 cm. The magnification produced by this microscope for normal adjustment is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For microscope at normal adjustment:
$M = \frac{L}{f_o} \times \frac{D}{f_e}$
where $L = 32$ cm, $f_o = 2$ cm, $f_e = 4$ cm, $D = 25$ cm
$M = \frac{32}{2} \times \frac{25}{4} = 16 \times 6.25 = 100$

subject: Physics
q: A fish looking up through the water sees the outside world contained in a circular horizon. If the refractive index of water is $4 / 3$ and the fish is $12 \mathrm{~cm}$ below the surface, the radius of this circle in $\mathrm{cm}$ is
question: A fish looking up through the water sees the outside world contained in a circular horizon. If the refractive index of water is $4 / 3$ and the fish is $12 \mathrm{~cm}$ below the surface, the radius of this circle in $\mathrm{cm}$ is
solution: $r=\frac{h}{\sqrt{\mu^2-1}}=\frac{36}{\sqrt{7}}$

subject: Physics
q: A thin plano convex lens made of glass of refractive index 1.5 is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.2. When the plane side of the lens is silver coated for complete reflection, the lens immersed in the liquid behaves like a concave mirror of focal length 0.2 m . The radius of curvature of the curved surface of the lens is
question: A thin plano convex lens made of glass of refractive index 1.5 is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.2. When the plane side of the lens is silver coated for complete reflection, the lens immersed in the liquid behaves like a concave mirror of focal length 0.2 m . The radius of curvature of the curved surface of the lens is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1.5}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{1.5-1.2}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{v}=\frac{1.5 \mathrm{R}}{0.3}=5 \mathrm{R} \\ & \frac{1.2}{\mathrm{f}}-\frac{1.5}{5 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{1.2-1.5}{-\mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{1.2}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{0.3}{\mathrm{R}} \times 2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}=2 \mathrm{R} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=0.1\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light is incident at an angle of incidence <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> on the glass slab of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>. After refraction, the light ray emerges out from other parallel faces and lateral shift between incident ray and emergent ray is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The thickness of the glass slab is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: A ray of light is incident at an angle of incidence <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> on the glass slab of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>. After refraction, the light ray emerges out from other parallel faces and lateral shift between incident ray and emergent ray is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The thickness of the glass slab is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
From snell's law, sinisinr=3⇒sin60°sinr=3⇒r=30°

From the diagram shown, d=tsini-rcosr⇒t×1232=43⇒t=12 cm

subject: Physics
q: Car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> overtakes another car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> at a relative speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. How fast will the image of car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> appear to move in the mirror of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> fitted in car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, when the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away from the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>?
question: Car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> overtakes another car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> at a relative speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. How fast will the image of car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> appear to move in the mirror of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> fitted in car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, when the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> away from the car <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>?
solution:
Object distance, u=−1.9 m=190 cm

Focal length, f=10 cm

Magnification, m=ff−u=1010−(−190)

Therefore, m=10200=120

Image velocity, VI=−m2V0=−1202×40

Hence, VI=−0.1 m s-1

Magnitude of velocity =0.1 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
The graph between 1u and 1v for a thin convex lens in order to determine its focal length is plotted as shown in the figure. The refractive index of lens is 1.5 and its both the surfaces have same radius of curvatures R. The value of R will be _____ cm.

(Where u= object distance, v= image distance)
question:
The graph between 1u and 1v for a thin convex lens in order to determine its focal length is plotted as shown in the figure. The refractive index of lens is 1.5 and its both the surfaces have same radius of curvatures R. The value of R will be _____ cm.

(Where u= object distance, v= image distance)
solution:
For point B, 1u=-0.10 cm-1, 1v=0

∴ Thus, u=-10 cm, v=∞

Using lens formula, 

1f=1v-1u=0--10

⇒f=10 cm

Now, using Lens maker's formula, 

1f=μ-11R1-1R2, where, μ is refractive index of lens.

⇒110 cm=1.5-12R=1R⇒R=10 cm
